serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of ricotta cheese is approximately 60g, equivalent to about 3 heaped tablespoons, commonly used as a spread, filling, or topping.

nutrient_source.title: Ricotta cheese, whole milk — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170851)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F01010021). HPB values used as primary where available; USDA SR Legacy used to supplement saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient data not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: Dairy foods, glycemic index and glycemic load — University of Sydney GI Database analogous dairy estimate; Atkinson FS et al. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI test for ricotta cheese is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Ricotta cheese contains 7.27g carbohydrate per 100g, predominantly lactose. Full-fat dairy products with high protein and fat content consistently show low GI values (GI 15–40 range) in the University of Sydney GI Database. An analogous estimate of GI 27 is applied, consistent with whole-milk dairy products (e.g., full-fat yogurt GI ~36, full-fat milk GI ~27 per Atkinson et al. 2021). Glycemic load per 60g serving calculated as: (27 × 7.27 × 0.60) / 100 = 1.18, rounded to 1.1. The high fat (10.18g/100g) and protein (7.54g/100g) content of ricotta further attenuates any glycemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (110mg/100g) with favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio; calcium supports vascular tone
hypertension.guidance: Ricotta cheese is suitable for hypertension management in moderate portions (50–75g per serving) due to its low sodium content (110mg/100g) and beneficial potassium (219mg/100g) and calcium (206mg/100g); however, saturated fat (~6.5g/100g) warrants portion control, and salted or flavoured varieties should be avoided.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low sugar (0.27g/100g), moderate carbohydrate (7.27g/100g), low GI (~27), high protein slows gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ricotta cheese is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management given its low sugar content (0.27g/100g), low glycemic index (~27), moderate carbohydrate load (7.27g/100g), and protein content (7.54g/100g) that attenuates post-prandial glucose response; plain unsweetened varieties should be chosen and portion size monitored to limit caloric intake from fat.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 6.47g/100g exceeds the 5g/100g threshold for cardiovascular concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ricotta cheese carries moderate cardiovascular concern due to its saturated fat content (~6.5g/100g), which can raise LDL cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portions to 50g per serving, prefer part-skim ricotta (saturated fat ~3.5g/100g), and account for ricotta within their total daily saturated fat budget of less than 10% of energy.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~6.5g/100g) exceeds 5g/100g RA threshold; calcium and protein benefit bone health in corticosteroid users
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ricotta cheese is not recommended as a regular staple for rheumatoid arthritis due to saturated fat (~6.5g/100g) that may promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity (TNF-alpha, IL-6); however, its calcium (206mg/100g) and protein content support bone health in patients on corticosteroids, and reduced-fat ricotta in 50–75g portions paired with anti-inflammatory foods is a reasonable compromise.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ricotta cheese is a fresh, soft Italian dairy product traditionally made by reheating the whey left over from the production of other cheeses, particularly from cow's milk. The name 'ricotta' means 'recooked' in Italian, reflecting this second-heating process. The curds that form are collected, drained, and lightly pressed to produce a moist, creamy white cheese with a mild, slightly sweet flavour and a grainy yet smooth texture. Nutritionally, ricotta provides 150 kcal per 100g, with 7.54g protein, 10.18g fat (of which approximately 6.5g is saturated), and 7.27g carbohydrate. It is a notable source of calcium (206mg/100g), potassium (219mg/100g), phosphorus (158mg/100g), vitamin A (117mcg RAE/100g), riboflavin (0.195mg/100g), and vitamin B12 (0.34mcg/100g). Its low sodium content (110mg/100g) and favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio make it suitable for blood pressure management. The low sugar content (0.27g/100g), low glycemic index (~27), and moderate carbohydrate load support glycaemic control in Type 2 Diabetes. Saturated fat content (~6.5g/100g) is the primary cardiovascular and inflammatory concern, making portion control and selection of part-skim varieties important for individuals with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis. Ricotta is widely used in Italian cuisine in dishes such as lasagne, cannoli, ravioli filling, and cheesecakes, and is also consumed as a spread or topping. It is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id F01010021, confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170851) for ricotta cheese whole milk. Both are approved primary sources. Saturated fat, fatty acid fractions, and extended micronutrient data supplemented from USDA SR Legacy where HPB did not report them. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database full-fat dairy benchmarks.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0g per 100g — confirmed zero per both HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; ricotta cheese contains no plant-derived fibre. Trans fat is 0.0g per 100g — confirmed trace/negligible per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin C is 0.0mg per 100g — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; ricotta is not a vitamin C source. Saturated fat value of 6.47g/100g sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170851) as HPB did not report fatty acid fractions. Monounsaturated fat (2.85g/100g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.32g/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy. Extended micronutrients (vitamins A, B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate, D, E; phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170851. Glycemic index set to 27 (low GI) as an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database full-fat dairy benchmarks (whole milk GI ~27, full-fat yogurt GI ~36 per Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care); no direct GI test for ricotta cheese exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 60g serving = 1.1. The input record listed 'low_calorie' as a dietary tag; this is debatable at 150 kcal/100g and has not been carried forward as a primary classification. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis listed suitable: false but the reasoning described a nuanced moderate-concern scenario; this has been reclassified as moderate concern with appropriate guidance. FoodOn URI, FoodEx2 code, and SNOMED CT code are cited as defensible matches based on published ontology documentation; reviewers should verify against current ontology releases.
